title: India's Basmati Rice Export in 2026: New Floor Prices, Top Markets, and Buyer Strategies
excerpt: India's government has revised the Minimum Export Price (MEP) for Basmati rice in 2026. We analyze how the new pricing floor affects importers in the Middle East, EU, and USA. With 1121 and 1718 varieties dominating, we break down current FOB rates per MT, container capacities, and the best procurement windows for buyers looking to lock in annual contracts. Key insight: 1718 Basmati is now outselling 1121 in several GCC markets due to superior aroma at competitive pricing.
author: Radiance Export Team

title: Global Cumin Shortage 2026: India's Rajasthan Crop Falls 30% — Impact on Importers
excerpt: Unseasonal rainfall in Rajasthan and Gujarat has damaged the 2026 cumin crop, with production estimates down 30% from last year. This is pushing cumin prices to multi-year highs across all grades — Singapore, Europe, and USA quality. We analyze the supply deficit, price forecasts through Q3 2026, alternative sourcing from Syria and Turkey, and recommend hedging strategies for bulk cumin importers. Volatile oil content and physical purity specs remain stable for available stock.
author: Radiance Spice Desk

title: Indian Specialty Coffee Exports Surge 40%: How Coorg and Chikmagalur Are Going Global
excerpt: India's specialty coffee exports grew 40% year-on-year in FY2025, driven by demand from European and Japanese roasters for single-origin, shade-grown Arabica from Coorg (Kodagu) and Chikmagalur. The Coffee Board of India reports that Arabica Plantation A and Peaberry grades are achieving premium pricing 20-30% above standard FOB rates. We profile the key growing regions, processing methods (washed vs natural), cup profiles, and how importers can source directly from estate-level producers through aggregators like Radiance Overseas.
author: Coffee Sourcing Team

title: EU's New Deforestation Regulation (EUDR) and Its Impact on Indian Agro Exports
excerpt: The European Union Deforestation Regulation (EUDR), effective from December 2025, requires importers to prove that agricultural products are deforestation-free. While India's agro exports — rice, spices, coffee, pulses — are largely from established agricultural land, the documentation requirements have changed. We explain what the EUDR means for Indian exporters, which product categories are affected, what due diligence documentation importers need, and how Radiance Overseas has adapted its traceability systems to ensure EUDR compliance for all EU-bound shipments.
author: Compliance Team

title: Indian Sesame Seeds Hit Record Export Volume: Tahini Demand Drives Growth
excerpt: India exported over 350,000 MT of sesame seeds in 2025, a new record driven by surging tahini and hummus consumption in the Middle East, Europe, and North America. Hulled sesame 99.95% purity remains the dominant export grade, with Gujarat and Rajasthan accounting for 85% of production. We analyze the price dynamics between Indian, Ethiopian, and Sudanese sesame, the impact of El Nino on the 2026 crop outlook, and quality benchmarks that buyers should specify for food-grade applications including FFA, moisture, and microbiological limits.
author: Radiance Oil Seeds Desk

title: Red Sea Crisis and Indian Agro Exports: Freight Rates, Delays, and Route Alternatives
excerpt: The ongoing Red Sea shipping disruption continues to impact Indian agro exports to Europe and the Mediterranean. Container vessels are rerouting via the Cape of Good Hope, adding 10-14 days transit time and $1,500-2,500 per TEU in additional freight costs. We analyze the impact on CIF pricing for rice, spices, and coffee shipments from JNPT and Mundra ports, the shift in buyer preference toward larger single shipments to amortize freight costs, and when the situation is expected to normalize. Alternative routing through Haifa and overland corridors is also discussed.
author: Logistics Team

title: Ashwagandha Extract Market Hits $1.5 Billion: India's Position as Global Supplier
excerpt: The global ashwagandha market is projected to reach $1.5 billion by 2027, and India controls over 80% of raw material supply. Madhya Pradesh and Rajasthan dominate production, with Nagori and regular varieties offering different withanolide concentrations. We examine the three key market segments — root powder (1.5% withanolides), concentrated extract (5%), and KSM-66 full-spectrum extract (>5%) — pricing differences, USDA organic certification requirements for the US supplement market, and why European buyers increasingly demand heavy metal and pesticide residue testing beyond standard NABL COA parameters.
author: Herbs & Pharma Desk

title: India's Chickpea Export Policy 2025-26: Kabuli vs Desi — What's Freely Exportable?
excerpt: India has updated its pulse export policy for FY2025-26, removing quantity restrictions on kabuli chickpeas while maintaining a floor price on desi chana. This policy shift is significant for Middle Eastern hummus manufacturers and EU food processors who rely on Indian 42/44 count kabuli chickpeas. We detail the current export regulations, available stock estimates from MP and Rajasthan, price comparison with Australian and Canadian chickpeas, and why Indian kabuli is gaining market share due to competitive pricing and improving quality grading infrastructure.
author: Pulses Export Team

title: NPOP to PGS-India: Understanding India's Organic Certification Landscape in 2025
excerpt: India operates two parallel organic certification systems — NPOP (National Programme for Organic Production) for third-party certified organic, and PGS-India (Participatory Guarantee System) for smallholder farmer groups. For international trade, only NPOP certification is recognized for export. We explain the critical differences, why importers must verify that their Indian supplier holds NPOP (not just PGS) certification, how the Transaction Certificate (TC) system works for each shipment, and the list of NPOP-accredited certifying bodies that EU and USDA recognize. Common compliance pitfalls that delay customs clearance are also covered.
author: Compliance Team

title: Dehydrated Onion and Garlic: India's $800 Million Export Opportunity
excerpt: India's dehydrated onion and garlic exports exceeded $800 million in FY2025, with Maharashtra's Nashik-Malegaon belt producing over 60% of global supply. The instant noodle, ready-meal, and seasoning industries are the primary demand drivers. We analyze the quality grades (minced, flakes, granules, powder), moisture and SO2 specifications that different markets require, the price advantage of Indian dehy products vs Chinese competitors, and why major food manufacturers like Nestle, Unilever, and ITC are increasingly sourcing from certified Indian producers. Infrastructure investments in Nashik's dehy processing cluster are creating new capacity for 2026.
author: Dehydrated Products Team

title: Non-Basmati Rice Export from India: IR 64, Swarna, and Parboiled Grades for African Markets
excerpt: Africa imports over 15 million MT of rice annually, and India is the dominant supplier of parboiled non-basmati grades. IR 64 Parboiled, PR 11, and Swarna are the workhorse varieties, shipping from JNPT and Mundra at 25 MT per 20-foot container. We break down the quality specifications (broken percentage, moisture, color) that Nigerian, Ghanaian, and Beninese importers require, current FOB and CIF pricing, the role of pre-shipment inspection (SGS/Bureau Veritas), and how India's non-basmati export policy has evolved since the 2023 ban. Demand from East African markets — Tanzania, Kenya, Madagascar — is the newest growth segment.
author: Rice Export Desk

title: Aflatoxin and Pesticide Residue Testing: MRL Compliance for Indian Agro Exports
excerpt: Maximum Residue Limits (MRLs) for pesticides and aflatoxin levels are the number one reason for shipment rejection at EU and US customs. The EU's Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF) flagged 47 Indian agro shipments in 2024 for MRL non-compliance — primarily spices and groundnuts. We explain the EU MRL database, US FDA detention thresholds, NABL lab testing protocols that exporters must follow, the critical difference between NABL and non-NABL certificates, and how Radiance Overseas implements a three-stage testing protocol (farm-gate, pre-processing, and pre-shipment) to ensure zero rejections. Specific MRL limits for turmeric, cumin, black pepper, and groundnuts are tabulated.
author: Quality Assurance Team

title: Indian Black Pepper: Malabar vs Tellicherry vs Vietnamese — A Buyer's Technical Comparison
excerpt: Indian black pepper commands a premium over Vietnamese pepper due to higher piperine content and distinctive flavor profile. But what exactly differentiates Malabar Grade 1 (MG1) from Tellicherry Extra Bold (TGSEB)? We provide a technical comparison covering berry size, bulk density, piperine percentage, volatile oil content, and moisture levels across Indian, Vietnamese, Brazilian, and Indonesian origins. For food manufacturers blending pepper for retail or food service, we explain which origin and grade delivers the best cost-per-unit-of-piperine ratio. Current pricing and crop outlook for the 2025-26 Kerala harvest season are included.
author: Spice Export Desk

title: Millets Are the New Quinoa: India's International Year of Millets Legacy Drives Export Growth
excerpt: The UN's International Year of Millets (2023) catalyzed lasting demand shifts. India — the world's largest millet producer — has seen pearl millet (bajra), finger millet (ragi), and sorghum (jowar) exports grow 65% since 2022. Health-conscious consumers in the EU, USA, and Japan are driving demand for gluten-free millet flour, millet-based snacks, and millet breakfast cereals. We analyze which millet varieties have the strongest export potential, target markets and buyer profiles, organic certification pathways, and pricing competitiveness versus quinoa and oats. India's government incentives for millet processing and export are also covered.
author: Radiance Export Team

title: Groundnut Export from India: Aflatoxin Compliance and How to Avoid EU RASFF Alerts
excerpt: Indian groundnuts are a $1 billion+ export category, but aflatoxin contamination remains the single biggest trade barrier. The EU's RASFF system flagged 23 Indian groundnut shipments in 2024 for aflatoxin B1 levels exceeding 2 ppb (total aflatoxin >4 ppb). We explain the science behind aflatoxin contamination in groundnuts, the critical role of pre-harvest and post-harvest management, how NABL-accredited lab testing with HPLC methodology differs from rapid test kits, and the specific sampling protocols (Commission Regulation 401/2006) that importers must understand. HPS (Hand Picked Selected) grade groundnuts with controlled cold chain storage consistently meet EU limits — we explain why.
author: Quality Assurance Team

title: Instant Coffee Export from India: Freeze-Dried Arabica Is the Fastest Growing Segment
excerpt: India's instant coffee exports crossed $900 million in FY2025, with freeze-dried Arabica growing at 25% annually. Russia, CIS countries, Southeast Asia, and Africa are the largest markets, but EU and USA demand for premium freeze-dried is accelerating. We compare spray-dried (powder/agglomerated) vs freeze-dried (crystal) production processes, pricing per MT, shelf life, solubility, and aroma retention characteristics. For private-label buyers, we outline the MOQ, lead time, custom blend ratios (Arabica/Robusta), and packaging options available from Indian manufacturers. India now ranks as the world's 5th largest instant coffee exporter.
author: Coffee Sourcing Team

title: Mundra Port Overtakes JNPT: India's New #1 Container Port for Agro Exports
excerpt: Adani's Mundra port in Gujarat has officially overtaken JNPT (Nhava Sheva) as India's largest container port by TEU volume. For agro exporters, this shift has significant implications — Mundra offers faster turnaround times, lower CFS charges, and better connectivity to Gujarat and Rajasthan origins (sesame, cumin, groundnut, castor). We compare Mundra vs JNPT vs Chennai across five parameters that matter for agro commodity exporters: transit time to key destinations, shipping line coverage, refrigerated container availability, CFS and port charges, and container availability. Our recommendation: use Mundra for oil seeds, spices, and cereals; JNPT for rice; Chennai for coffee and South Indian products.
author: Logistics Team

title: Moringa Powder Export: EU Novel Food Regulation and How Indian Suppliers Are Navigating It
excerpt: Moringa oleifera leaf powder is technically classified as a 'novel food' under EU Regulation 2015/2283, which means it requires pre-market authorization for sale in the EU. However, enforcement varies by member state, and several Indian exporters have successfully placed moringa products through the 'traditional food from third country' notification pathway. We explain the regulatory status in the EU, UK (post-Brexit rules differ), USA (GRAS status), and Australia/NZ, the required documentation for each market, and how organic certification (EU Organic + USDA NOP) strengthens the regulatory position. Current pricing, protein and iron content specifications, and major EU buyer profiles are included.
author: Herbs & Pharma Desk

title: Toor Dal Prices Soar 45%: India's Production Shortfall Creates Export Opportunity for Lentils
excerpt: India's domestic toor dal (pigeon pea) prices have risen 45% in 2025 due to consecutive poor monsoon seasons in Maharashtra and Karnataka. While this has triggered government intervention in the domestic market, it has simultaneously opened an export window for masoor dal (red lentils) and moong dal, which are in surplus. For international buyers, this creates an arbitrage opportunity — Indian masoor dal is currently 15-20% cheaper than Canadian red lentils on a CIF basis for Middle Eastern and South Asian destinations. We analyze the export policy implications, available surplus estimates, quality specifications, and why lentil importers should act before the next kharif season changes the equation.
author: Pulses Export Team

title: Private Label Packaging for Indian Agro Products: What Importers Need to Specify
excerpt: An increasing number of European and American importers are sourcing Indian rice, spices, and pulses for their own private label brands. But the specification process is complex — packaging material (PP, BOPP, jute, vacuum, nitrogen-flushed), labeling regulations (EU FIC 1169/2011, FDA 21 CFR 101), barcode formats (EAN-13 vs UPC-A), nutritional panel requirements, allergen declarations, and country-of-origin labeling all vary by destination market. We provide a complete specification checklist that importers should share with their Indian supplier, common mistakes that cause customs delays, and the typical MOQ and lead time for private label orders. Radiance Overseas offers private label packaging from 500g retail packs to 50kg bulk bags with custom branding.
author: Export Documentation Team

title: Sella vs Steam vs Raw Basmati: A Visual and Technical Guide for First-Time Rice Importers
excerpt: If you are importing Indian Basmati rice for the first time, the terminology can be confusing. What exactly is the difference between Raw (white milled), Sella (parboiled), and Steam (lightly steamed) Basmati? This comprehensive guide explains the processing differences with visual comparisons, how each type behaves during cooking (elongation ratio, stickiness, texture), which markets prefer which type (Middle East prefers Sella, EU/USA prefer Raw, South Asia prefers Steam), pricing differences per MT, and shelf life considerations. We cover this for all four major Basmati varieties: 1121, 1718, Pusa, and 1509. Container loading capacity and standard quality specifications (broken %, moisture, whiteness) for each processing type are included in a downloadable comparison table.
author: Rice Export Desk

title: India's Basmati Rice Export Crosses $5 Billion in FY2025
excerpt: India's Basmati rice exports have surged past the $5 billion mark in FY2025, driven by strong demand from GCC countries, the EU, and the USA. We break down the numbers and what it means for importers.
author: Radiance Export Team

excerpt: GMP+ Feed Safety Assurance is mandatory for exporting animal feed to the EU. We explain the certification process, audit requirements, and how Radiance maintains compliance.
